comment.body: Hi Alma, thanks for all the analysis. Let’s say that EU agrees to a ceasefire, Holy Alliance is able to form, and tariff (even those with China) has dropped, is there any other way the US market wouldn’t be out of the woods? No tariff so no stagflationary force. But without a further drop, wouldn’t that mean the ME and Russian funds wouldn’t be able to buy out China’s stake in US tech companies?

comment.body:
As I wrote in Friday’s post, China and America will hold talks. The crux is whether they can strike a deal so that China steps back from closer cooperation with the EU, or not. 
If yes, then the China/US tariff war ends, and the US market can slowly start moving up. 
If not, then the Fed still has to defend, and the risk remains.

comment.body:
So, those of you following me know that I’ve been anticipating a supportive OpEx for a few weeks now.
Especially now that Trump and Xi are about to talk.
(If you’ve been watching the news, you should know Xi has been saying for a while they should sit down to negotiate—they did an easing move first.)
Since Wednesday, the Fed has been stepping in as a buyer, albeit in small amounts so it wouldn’t be too obvious. By now it’s clear. I mentioned here in the chat last time that QE is coming soon... officially.
Additionally, an American delegation is heading to Saudi Arabia, because Arab and Russian investors want their confidence back in Trump’s ability to manage the situation and keep it all on track. Trump himself won’t go until May—for now, it’s just delegates.

comment.body: It all comes down to what Alma is saying, that Trump is using all this tariff hokuspokus just to bring everyone to negotiate. Markets crashing was not his generic intention. Even at zero tariff rates, there are hidden trade barriers like quality controls, ie. EU saying we won‘t import grains that were grown using fossil-fueled fertilizers, or that US cars don‘t meet EU safety standards and the like. So it is basically all about forcing ppl to the negotiating table
